vulnerabilities.title: Unrestricted mintTo/burnTo let attackers front-run and steal pending pool deposits/redemptions
vulnerabilities.summary: `InitCore.mintTo` and `InitCore.burnTo` are public and do not bind staged assets to the caller. In `LendingPool.mint`, shares are minted from `newBalance - cash` (all unaccounted underlying currently sitting in the pool). In `LendingPool.burn`, redemption burns `balanceOf(address(this))` (all pool-share tokens currently sitting in the pool). This lets any external caller claim someone else's pending staged deposit or staged redemption by calling first.

description.desc: mintTo/burnTo are publicly callable, while LendingPool accounts deposits/redemptions from pool-global staged balances rather than caller-scoped amounts

vulnerabilities.impact: Direct theft of user assets staged for lending deposit or redemption (and any other flow that stages pool shares before burn), resulting in full loss of those assets.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept:
1) Victim transfers underlying to a lending pool, intending to call `mintTo` in a later transaction.
2) Attacker front-runs with `InitCore.mintTo(pool, attacker)`.
3) The pool mints shares for the victim's full staged amount to attacker.
4) Victim's later `mintTo` sees zero remaining delta and receives nothing/reverts.
5) Likewise, if victim transfers pool shares to the pool before calling `burnTo`, attacker front-runs `burnTo(pool, attacker)` and receives the redeemed underlying for all staged shares.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Replace staged-balance public flows with amount-parameterized deposit/redeem functions that perform transfer and accounting atomically in one call. If staged semantics are retained, bind claims to the original stager or restrict who can execute `mintTo`/`burnTo` for staged balances.

vulnerabilities.title: collateralizeWLp can be front-run to steal pending wLP NFT collateral deposits
vulnerabilities.summary: The wLP collateral path requires the wrapped-LP NFT to be in `PosManager` custody before `InitCore.collateralizeWLp` can succeed. `PosManager.addCollateralWLp` only checks that `ownerOf(tokenId) == address(this)`, the token is not already marked collateralized, and `balanceOfLp(tokenId) != 0`. It does not verify who transferred that NFT into `PosManager` or which position it was intended for. Because `InitCore.collateralizeWLp` is publicly callable by any user for their own authorized position,

description.desc: addCollateralWLp credits any eligible wLP token already held by PosManager to whichever authorized position calls first, without binding that token to the original depositor

vulnerabilities.impact: Direct theft of user wLP NFT collateral (and underlying LP value/rewards) when deposit and collateralize are not bound atomically to the depositor's position.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept:
1) Victim transfers a valid wLP NFT (`tokenId`) to `PosManager`, intending to collateralize it to their position in a subsequent transaction.
2) Attacker watches mempool, creates/uses a compatible position, and front-runs with `InitCore.collateralizeWLp(attackerPos, wLp, tokenId)`.
3) `PosManager.addCollateralWLp` accepts the token (already owned by `PosManager`) and marks it collateralized under attacker position.
4) Victim's transaction reverts (`ALREADY_COLLATERALIZED` / ownership mismatch in later flow).
5) Attacker decollateralizes/unwinds the stolen wLP collateral and extracts underlying LP value.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Introduce depositor-to-token binding for incoming wLP NFTs (e.g., record intended position in `onERC721Received` data and enforce it in `collateralizeWLp`), or add a single atomic core entrypoint that transfers the wLP NFT and collateralizes within the same call while rejecting unsolicited custodial transfers.
